Hi, this is Oren and we’re now beginning the module O for Offer
the Prize. What this module is about is how to introduce the
commercial terms of your deal. Think about it this way, we as the
pitchers, the pitchmen, pitchwomen, have just invested a large
amount of time setting the frame for the audience. That’s when
we take control of the social environment, we’ve raised our own
status, we’ve let people know we’re the prize, that we’re going to
be interviewing them to see if they’re good customers, that
they’re going to respect us as sellers and they’re going to earn
their way into our deal.
Then we told the story. We gave a compelling pitch that was 20
minutes or less and then at the end of the pitch we realized we
were running thin on attention so we used intrigue to reboot the
mind of the audience or recapture their attention. And that was R,
Revealing the Intrigue. So we’ve been through S, Set the Frame, T,
Tell the Story, R, Reveal the Intrigue, and now we’re going to move
in to O, Offer the Prize.
You see, as much as we want people to understand that they
need to be a good customer and well-behaved and that we don’t
want to take counter party risks. They need to earn their way into
our deal. Their cash is a commodity. What we have is very
valuable. They also need to understand our time is short. Our deal
horizon is finite. And there’s a time constraint for them making a
decision. And we are interviewing them as much as they are
interviewing us. The audience needs to prove to us that they’re
good buyers, they’re a good audience, they’d be good partners.
But among all this, we still want to close deals, we want to get
money and we want to land accounts. We’re here to pitch and sell
ultimately, close deals, get money, land accounts. And in order to
do that we need to offer them terms, the price, the delivery
schedule, the payment terms, the options, all kinds of
commercial terms. For example, if we were going to help you raise
money to sell your company, we’d have to tell you what our
commission is, what we get for raising the money.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	1
Oren Klaff:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 18 --

So if we raised $5 million for you, we’d have to tell you our price
for doing that is 3 percent or 5 percent. We’d have to tell you how
long it takes and how much cash you would have to put up front.
And if I was selling you blue plastic frogs, I would have to tell you
how much they cost by the dozen, where the price breaks are for
the container and I have to offer you the extra option of, I don't
know, a dozen pink squirrels at $5,000 each to keep the blue
frogs safe in winter. Whatever, you get my point.
So as much as we have framed ourselves as a power player
through prizing, as much as we use frame control to raise our
status and our local star power, we now have to offer the prize. In
a moment we’ll cover exactly how to do this. As we move into O,
Offer the Prize, we’re going to see some very interesting dynamics
and to understand those dynamics we need to take a look at
where we came from, where we are and where we’re going.
So to familiarize you with this chart that we’re working on here
and a lot's going to happen within this diagram, but obviously you
can see this goes from the starting point, which is Set the Frame.
It’s going to move into Tell the Story. Next we’re going to do
Reveal the Intrigue and then Offer the Prize, which is where we
are now. Beyond that what you’re going to see in the future is
Nailing the Hook Point and then, of course, the objective of all of
this which is Getting the Deal.
So let’s clear the screen now. What’s interesting, and I want to call
your attention to, as we progress through this you’re going to see
some things happening to status. So in Set the Frame, you can
imagine you walk in the door, get on the phone, get somebody to
your web site, they open an email, whatever it is, you’re starting
with very low status. That’s the point of all this, right, prizing,
setting the frame, doing these things, is to raise status. So during
the Setting the Frame stage you are raising status so you can give
your pitch from a higher status position. Maybe you do better
than this, maybe you do worse, but you definitely are going to see
your social status raise as you set the frame strongly.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	2
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 18 --

Then as you move into giving the pitch, that’s going to be your
maximum status. You gave a great story. You set yourself up as an
interesting character. You told an amazing story and a pitch about
your project or your deal and this is the point where you’re at the
highest status. That’s why we say at the end of the pitch you’re
able to put counter-pressure on your audience. You’re able to
make them qualify themselves back to you. You’re able to ask
them why should I let you in my deal. Why should I take you as a
customer? And you can do that because here your status is so
high. Now I’ll point to it here on the screen. At this point your
status is so high that you’re able to ask those kinds of questions.
Why would I take you in the deal? You can see if I fill it in here, this
is exactly the point that I’m talking about, where it is right here.
Now let’s move on. So then as you move from Telling the Story,
we’ve got maximum status. Now people’s attention is going to
drop a little bit at the end. They’re tired, you’ve used their
attention for 20 minutes and then you’re going to see something
like this. During Reveal the Intrigue your status is going to bounce
around because they’re tired, you’re trying to reboot their
attention, something hard to do, but to the degree you succeed
you’ll get your status back. So it will drop high and then go low
again. It will bounce around.
Then, and here’s what I really want to call your attention to, in this
module we’re in now, Offer the Prize, it’s really going to drop. It
may drop lower than this because you’re now offering the deal.
You’re asking them to sign up for something. You’re telling them
commercial terms. You’re giving them the price. You’re going to
see your status drop when you try and get resources from
someone. If you think about it, this ties back in some ways to our
ancient brain or ancient mind. A hundred thousand years ago
when one human walked up to another human and said I want
something that you have, created fear because resources were so
constrained in those times that anybody trying to get your
resources causes fear inside you. In this same way when you are
starting to act needy and you’re going to act a little bit needy
because you need to sell them this product, this idea, this project.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	3
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 18 --

When you start to act needy, offer the prize, make an offer, give
the price, you’re going to see your status drop. And then what’s
good is when you start to Nail the Hook Point, which is module
after this, your status is going to go way back up and that’s going
to be a great time. So one way to visualize this would be maybe to
look at this wave here. In the beginning you’re going to come with
low status, it’s going to accelerate and you’re going to end the
frame setting period with pretty high status. Then you’re going to
give your pitch. It’s really going to climb up high. This is going to
be your peak status right here. That is when you’re going to apply
the counter-pressure and say why would I ever let you in my deal,
with everything I have going, without knowing more about you?
Then you’re going to burn down their attention and really have
done a lot of emotional things and your status is going to drop. It
might drop precipitously, like I’ve shown here, but you’re going to
reveal some intrigue and get to climb back up. That’s the job of
intrigue to get attention and status back. Then in the module
we’re in, we’re going to see status drop again because we are
giving commercial terms and price and we’re starting to ask for
stuff. And then in Nail the Hook Point it’s going to waver around a
little bit. So if I overlay all of this you can see how it looks. What I
love about this worksheet and looking at it in this format is you
can really see how the modules build on each other and how they
interplay with status.
So let’s put this status line back up. Then let’s look at the modules
by name, Set the Frame, Tell the Story, Reveal the Intrigue. Let’s
just leave those three up. And then let’s go ahead and look at the
elements within Set the Frame. So now, if I put the arrow over
here, you can see, of course, we’ve discussed many times,
prizing, the sense of walking in the room with no status, no frame
control, nothing. The other person, the buyer, the guy who makes
the decision is having all the power. And then converting the
power frame over to us by saying we’re in control of this meeting.
We’re in control of the agenda. You’re the buyer. All you have is
money. We have this product, this idea, this venture. It’s
incredibly valuable and we could work with anybody.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	4
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 18 --

We want to choose you, but of course we’re going to have to hear
about you. So we’re going to give our presentation. All those
elements of prizing that we’ve talked about. We’re certainly going
to give the big idea. We talked about the big idea many times. If
you could coat ball bearings with Teflon and Kevlar and really
slippery oil, which has never been done before, you can triple the
speed at which machinery works. And if you could do that, you
could grow a billion dollar company in just the United States’
domestic market in under five years. That’s what we’re here to
show you today; the possibility of doing that. So you’re going to
give the big idea. This is all under frame setting. While you’re doing
that you’re going to be establishing frame control. As you
establish frame control, your status is going to be reaching new
heights and right when your status is as high as can get in the
frame control segment, then you’re going to move on. You’re
going to have maximum attention.
When it’s time, very early in the meeting, you’re going to move
into your pitch. So let’s see what that looks like. Within the pitch
of course you’re going to do these things that we’ve talked about
over and over, build a compelling character, give the big idea,
really spend some time deepening the problem, making it painful,
making sure it’s understood how the range of this problem, how
real human beings have this very deep problem and it causes
problems in their lives. And then you’re going to say, "Hey, we’re
not the smartest, greatest, best guys on earth. Other people can
solve this. All right? But we have a solution that’s pretty damn
good. Better than all the other stuff out there, that’s for sure and
we’ll show you how it works."
So that would be a good 20 minute pitch. Of course there’s other
stuff to do in the pitch, but if you did just that, you could be quite
successful. And then we know your status here is going to be very
high if you’ve done a great pitch. And people are going to start
acting weird. They’re going to try and status correct because
you’ve raised your status so high by giving a structured,
interesting, compelling, funny, exciting pitch narrative that
includes human stories.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	5
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 18 --

Because you’ve done all this, the audience is going to want to give
their own stories with that same great narrative, but they don’t
know how to do it. They don’t know the underlying structure. And
so they’re going to be weird about it. They’re going to ask you
questions to try and lower your status. So they might say, "Well,
we heard that the Teflon wears off the ball bearings within a year,"
or "Many other people have tried to build that kind of company
and have failed." So they’re going to try and ask you those
questions. Explain yourself. But you’re not going to chase those
conversation threads.
But now you know you’ve kept somebody’s attention for over 20
minutes and they’re fatigued. So now we’re going to have to
reboot their mind with intrigue. And here’s where before we can
continue and offer the prize, which is the module we’re in now,
we’ve got to reveal some more intrigue. Status is going to be
moving all over the map. It’s certainly going to be dropping just
because people’s attention is waning. So we’re going to
remember the three principles of the human condition right? Of
course we know that humans have three emotions, angry, hungry
and horny. But really, how that plays out is we want that which
we cannot have, we chase that which moves away from us and
we only value those things in which we pay for them.
So when we deploy some intrigue, it’s going to take advantage of
those things. You can’t have it. You have to apply for it and there’s
more to our product you haven’t seen. You can’t have the
premium product. So you’re going to really provide some intrigue
at that point. And after intrigue, there’s the module we’re in now,
which is Offer the Prize. And offering the prize is really hard. That’s
why status is going to drop, because now we want something.
Now we want something from the other people and so we’re
going to be talking about price and terms and contract, and really
moving product out of our factory or out of our servers into the
customers’, making them pay for it, timeline. And there’s a
process for buying. This is all hard stuff to talk about because this
is where really a transaction is going to happen around. Terms,
and price, that’s offering the prize. And then after that we’re going
to nail the hook point which is really all about calibration and we’ll
get to that next.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	6
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 18 --

I want you to see this build one more time and see how much
you’ve accomplished in such a short time. So we’re going to put
the status build back in there. Up there’s the highest point in Tell
the Story. You move into Reveal and Intrigue it’s going to bounce
around and then in Offer the Prize you’ve got to work hard
because you’re going to be in that low status period. And let’s
watch what happens, we build Set the Frame, all those things
prizing, the big idea, time constraints, getting attention, getting
the room or the call under frame control. And we’ll leave it up
there and watch what happens. Now, key in Tell the Story, give the
pitch across 20 minutes, be prepared for the questions that are
going to come at you, build your character, deliver the problem,
explain the solution and how it works. You’re going to reveal
intrigue right after that. Look how much you’ve accomplished and
how much you’re doing. So it’s pretty amazing to see it all build
across time.
And then we’re frame stacking and then move into nailing the
hook point and getting the deal. And this is the pitch process from
start to finish. But don’t get overwhelmed by this page. You’re just
going to break it down into whatever module you’re in and take it
piece by piece. Again, if you’re in just giving the pitch, this is all
you have to deal with, building the character, explaining the
problem, giving your solution and how it works. So don’t get
overwhelmed by this whole map of things you have to do; within
each module you know exactly what to do.
Okay, now let’s get past the schematic here and really get into the
steps we have to take to offer the prize. So far, I have showed you
the first three phases or modules of the pitch, Setting the Frame,
Telling the Story, Revealing the Intrigue. And so by now you’ve
held the audience or the buyer’s attention for a while. The buyer,
the audience knows the essentials of what you have, who you are,
why you’re a great character, why this idea is important, and how
it works. He knows what your value proposition is and he gets
exactly what the big idea is. He knows when he buys, what he’s
going to get.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	7
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 18 --

But, of course, in a great pitch you have to do more than just
show and tell. This is a sale. This is a business development. We’re
here to make a deal happen, to make money transact. So now
towards the end of the pitch, you only have a few minutes left to
propose something concrete, an actionable, a sale, a transaction,
a deal, terms. You have to propose price, the delivery schedule,
the installation, however it works in your business, minimum
order volume, how the transaction works.
So welcome to the next phase, Offering the Prize, offer the terms
in a way that’s hot. Offer the terms in a way that's hot and I call
that a hot cognition. So let’s go through now exactly what a hot
cognition is and then how to offer the terms in a hot cognition.
Part of this is revisiting the ideas of frame control and the idea
that the pitch is done in a social way, in a social environment and
get frame control and your perspective becomes the dominant
perspective because the audience or the buyer can like your deal
or be afraid of it before he or she knows even much detail about
it. And he can probably decide yes or no without even knowing
exactly what it is. That is a hot cognition. That’s what hot
cognitions do. Deciding something you like before you fully
understand it, that is a hot cognition. And that is why in our pitch
we don’t go into much detail and when we get the analytical
questions, we defer them to later because we want this to be
understood and we want it to be felt by the audience in a hot way
not in a cold way, not in a cold reasoned analysis, but in a hot
cognition.
Think about it in terms of your own life. What do you do?
Research, analyze and then decide. Let’s say you’re going to buy a
car. Step one, research. Step two, analyze your research. Step
three, decide which one you’re going to get. So if we were all
computer-like or even behaved like economists think we do, it
would work this way, but we are not analytical by nature. It
doesn’t work like that; research, analyze, decide. Sometimes we
think we’ve thought it through or we’ve used the decision matrix.
We think we’re smart, careful and rational decision makers but
really we buy what we want. In decision making, we don’t do
much analysis if any at all. We go with our gut. You know Jack
Welsh from GE, we all know the famous manager who built a huge
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	8
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 18 --

amount of value in that company and when he wrote his
biography, as I wrote in my book as I wrote about him, he didn’t
call it intense analysis by Jack Welsh, it was called Straight from
the Gut.
There’s a whole side to us as humans that computers don’t have.
Our bodies and our minds, we just know in our gut, we know the
situations that we meet in life and how we should respond and
what we want. Those are hot cognitions. You know the car that
you want. If your research and your analysis and your decisions
are that you should get a station wagon, but you go see Volvo’s
new sport wagon, you immediately throw all the research out and
say that’s what I want. I’m paying for it, that’s what my gut tells
me to get.
I say it in this way because this is what is important to create in
the audience, this feeling of wanting. And this idea of wanting is
such an interesting subject to me and I hope to you, as well. Think
about you and your friends. You didn’t become friends with each
other because you did some kind of analysis. You didn’t choose
your career because you analyzed what in ten years is going to be
the highest paying career. And you don’t choose what sports
you’re going to watch out of some kind of detailed analysis, the
pros and cons of each sporting situation. If you really stop to think
about it, most major decisions in your own life are not made by
cold, reasoned, analytical, cognitive processes. You don’t do
evaluation and analysis, but instead the sports you watch, the
people you love, the friends that you have those are hot
cognitions, probably the car you drive and the city you live in.
That’s what you wanted.
There are probably very few decisions in your own life that aren’t
hot, even stuff that you should be thoughtful about like what
computer you should buy. Now I’ve got this MacBook Pro here
and I love this machine, but probably the PC that I switched out of
is a better computer, but damn, this MacBook Pro is beautiful and
I just wanted it when I saw it. So I’m sure in your own life if you
think about it, very few decisions that you made analytically.
Those are hot cognitions. I say it in this way so you know what to
recognize when I say a hot cognition because now we’re going to
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	9
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 18 --

move it out of your personal experience into your business life
and your business relationships.
Even in your business relationships most of the time, the data you
have collected about the choices and alternatives and the options
that you use to buy a copier or to lease some office space or get
some fleet trucks, you didn’t use those analytics to make the
decision anyway. You used them to justify decisions after the fact.
As I said before, we buy the cars we like, we lease the office space
that we like or want, we choose employees, even with all kinds of
analysis we choose the ones we like. Maybe they’re the ones we
find attractive from a personality standpoint.
And then we justify these choices to other people with all the
facts and explanations and rational analysis that we did. We buy
what we want, the copier, the lease, the fleet car, the CFO, the
employee we want and then we rationalize those choices with the
research we did. We don’t need facts and explanations to
convince ourselves. We know what we like. Even when we try the
rational approach of making lists of pros and cons, and this is my
summary point, when we try the rational approach and we make
those lists and we do the spreadsheets and we do the analysis, if
it does not come out how we like, we go back and we re-do the
analysis until it comes out the way we want it. And that is hot
cognition.
Ultimately, if we want to create that hot cognition now when we
offer the price and terms of our deal, we want to create hot
cognition in the buyer and the audience. How do we do that?
We’re going to reveal the terms of the deal within a four-frame
hot cognition stack. We’re going to reveal the terms of the deal
within a four-frame hot cognition stack. To make it work you just
implement the frames you’re already familiar with from the Set
the Frame module. All you have to do is learn how to stack them
up, one after the other, to generate this hot cognition. In other
words to create what I call wanting. We’re not trying to get the
buyer to like us because learning to like something is slow and it’s
intellectual and it requires analysis and it all happens up in the
neocortex. This is not the business I’m in, getting people to like
me, or getting people to like my deal.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	10
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 18 --

I want high temperature frames that create hot cognitions that
create hot wanting. And I use frame stacks to make sure that the
mind and the brain of the person that I’m pitching to wants what I
have, wants to work with me, moves towards me, ends up
chasing me in a hot cognition in order to get the deal.
So here are the four frames we’re going to stack in quick
succession to introduce the terms of the deal. And if you do this
correctly by the way, you’re going to easily move into the last
module, the last part of the pitch, the hook point. So the four
frames, and you can use many more, but these are the four that I
found very effective. The first frame in your frame stack that will
help you create the hot cognition is the Intrigue Frame, and I’m
going to show you in a moment how to use the Intrigue Frame to
introduce price. The second will be the Prize Frame, and we’ll use
the Prize Frame to introduce terms. The third would be the Time
Frame, and I will use in the example the Time Frame to talk about
minimum order volumes, introduce the term of minimum order
volume. Then the fourth will be the Moral Authority Frame. And
we’ll use the stack in the fourth, the Moral Authority Frame, to tie
it all together and complete my four-frame stack.
Of course, the hardest part of making the sale, closing the deal, is
introducing price because everybody wants to pay a lower price.
And I might say it like this, "John, the price is more than you want
to pay of course, how could it ever be different, and it’s less than
we want to charge. The blue plastic frogs are a thousand dollars
each. There’s no way around that. They’re actually a thousand and
one dollars, that’s just the price. But it’s super-interesting, you
are going to meet, with the order size that you have to place, for
the size of the fields that you have to put these frogs in for your
insect defense system, because of the size of order you have
you’re going to meet our chief engineer. This guy is amazing. He’s
the guy that picks the blue. He flies to Afghanistan twice a year
and goes out with a military escort to those areas, because
Afghanistan, and look it up, is the only place in the world where
real crystalline blue grows naturally in volume enough to create
this blue color. If you grow this blue color in the lab it doesn’t
come out right and the insects aren’t afraid of it. But look, you’ve
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	11
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 18 --

got to meet the engineer. This guy's going to be your instant
friend and when he describes the manufacturing process it is so
amazing, the most fascinating thing you’ll ever learn. So anyway
with the first delivery I’m going to bring him along, he’s going to
help with the install and you’re going to love the engineering
team, and specifically Rolf who is the lead engineer and he’s the
one that sources the blue." So the Intrigue Frame can be done just
a simply as that.
Then without pause we’ll move into the Prize Frame. Now as you
know, with this order of blue plastic frogs you need to get an
irrigation report and these are $25,000. Now fortunately, in my
previous life I was an irrigation consultant and I have all the
models, I still have the license. They’re kind of time consuming to
do, these irrigation reports and although I sign maybe 20 or 30
new customers a month, I only do five irrigation reports. I’ve
already done four this month because they’re just time
consuming, they’re extremely valuable. It’s probably going to cost
you $25 grand to get one, but I think, and if I look at my order
book and I remember this stuff, you’re going to be my fifth
customer and I’m going to do the irrigation report for you because
you’ve asked to place an order of this size. I do this for very few
people. I don’t really care about the money, but if you want to
work with me, this is the kind of stuff that I bring to the table.
When you buy this order from Blue Plastic Frog Industries, what
you get is me. You’re going to find that’s incredibly valuable both
economically and also as a resource for your business. So you’re
going to get that and we’re going to do that together.
You know, then of course, I’ll move without pause to the Time
Frame. The blue plastic frogs are funny, especially this time of
year because this is when our inventories go low. Look, this isn’t
me, this is not a tactic. The French buy these things up, I don’t
know why, but this time of year inventories get below 10,000 and
they are dropping low. When it gets below 10,000 there is a price
trigger and the price goes up 33 percent. There’s nothing I can do
about it at that point and I’m just telling you right now you have
about a week to finalize this order or a price increase is going to
get triggered and you’re not going to be able to come crying to me
and saying I want the previous price. That is the real time frame.
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	12
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 18 --

And I’m not saying you have to buy today, or tomorrow, but I am
saying there is a realistic time frame in which this deal has to get
done or we’re both going to be singing the blues about the price.
And then next, of course, we’ll move into the Moral Authority
Frame without pause, and this will be the fourth in our four-
frame stack. I think it could go something like this, you know, the
blue plastic frogs that we manufacture are the best in the world
as we’ve been talking about. They’re five gold star, EEE LEED
rating. They’re the highest environmental rating in the world of all
irrigation and insect deterrent manufacturers we have the highest
environmental manufacturing and highest environmental quality
rating.
So one problem is we find companies like you buy our product in
order to demonstrate to regulators that you’re environmentally
friendly. But we don’t like you using us a veneer or a patina or just
a casual demonstration of environmental friendliness. We’ve got
to make sure that you guys actually are environmentally friendly
throughout, that we’re not your only environmentally friendly
effort.
So you have to prove to us what other environmental programs
you have running before we let you place an order of this size
because it’s important to us that our customers are real and care
about the environment and they don’t just use a small order with
us or a large order with us to frame themselves as
environmentally friendly. They really have to be that way
throughout the organization, and that’s something we’re going to
need you to prove to us. So that will be a good example of the
Moral Authority Frame.
So now we’ve delivered the Intrigue Frame around price, the Prize
Frame around terms, the Time Frame around when a decision has
to be made and the Moral Authority Frame to tie it all together.
And don’t think you have to over think this four-frame hot
cognition stack, the four-frame stack, because you’ve done
everything else so well up to this point you can actually do it quite
calmly. Hey, yes the price is a thousand dollars each, but guess
what as part of this you’re going to be meeting the engineers and
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	13
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 18 --

these guys travel to Afghanistan and they buy this stuff and they
have these incredible adventures.
You’re going to start to meet this team. You’re certainly going to
get to work with me and I’m unlike the other sales reps of
irrigation and insect deterrent equipment. I’m an irrigation
consultant. I add tons of value. I care about my customers and I’m
probably the most knowledgeable rep in the world about this
product and how to deter insects and that’s something I’m really
glad you took the effort to convince me to sell these to you
because we’re going to get to work together. You’re getting a lot
in this transaction. And by the way, there’s no free delivery and
there’s no free installation, but you do get to work with me and
that’s a huge benefit. And look, the time frame, you don’t have to
decide today. I never want anybody to decide in a single day but
I’m telling you right now the French buy this stuff this time of
year, stock levels drop, the price goes up.
If you ask me, and I’ve been doing this for ten years, if you don’t
want to get a 33 percent price kick in the summer season you
have about a week to decide. And, look, the last thing is you have
to be honest about your intent for these things. We know we have
the best environmental rating in the world. It’s one of our features
and it’s one of the reasons that it’s about a thousand dollars and
maybe that’s a little bit more than the next guys. But you cannot,
we will not allow you and you have to tell me right now if this is
your intent, we won’t allow you to buy these things to show to
regulators that you’re environmentally friendly if the rest of your
program isn’t EEE or EEEE rated. And if you’re not trying to get
your LEED accreditation for environmental friendliness. You see
the four-frame stack can be done if a fairly calm way and you’ll
find frames of your own and add them in, but it’s important to put
them one after the other and that’s when you’ll introduce the
price, the terms, the order volume and all those kinds of things.
Let’s talk a little bit more about hot versus cold cognition. I want
to revisit that for another minute. As I once wrote, maybe the best
way to define a hot versus cold cognition is to compare it to
chocolate and spinach because you know the cold, hard facts.
Spinach is good for your and it has a lot of nutrients and you
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	14
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 18 --

should be eating more of it. I’m telling you right now, you should
be eating more of it. I should. But when you or I are offered a
piece of chocolate instead, we go for it. That’s the difference
between hot and cold cognition. We know spinach is good for us,
but we go for the chocolate.
And that’s the test of whether your pitch goes well. Does the
audience or the buyer, do they want it, do they want to buy it, do
they want to be involved with you, do they want to invest in you,
and your idea and be your partner. How much thinking about your
presentation does your audience need before he or she forms a
preference about it. How fully and completely must an idea be
presented and thought about and how much rational analysis
needs to be completed before the audience decides good or bad?
Most of your pitch right now, you’ve done a great job of this.
You’ve focused on emotion and characters and you’ve got
structure and their structured around problem and solution. And
when analytical questions have come at you, you’ve deferred
them. So you’ve done a good job at really creating the hot
cognitions but now in Offer the Prize, you’re giving terms and
price and there’s some risk. You don’t want to wait for an
evaluation of the price or your target, your buyer will wander off
into cold cognition and think about the terms and start to
evaluate. So by stacking the four frames and triggering hot
cognitions you create a more instantaneous evaluation and the
reaction of wanting.
Just a couple more thoughts about hot cognition. Hot cognition is
the inner certainty of knowing something by feeling it. A cold
cognition is a certainty of knowing something by evaluating it. But
hot cognitions are extremely fast. Hot cognitions develop through
these ancient brain structures, through the stem and the mid-
brain, right near the crocodile brain. Cold cognitions are slow,
they’re analytical, they develop in the neocortex. Cold cognitions
are calculated and they take time to suggest a solution. This is
how the neocortex goes about its business. It collects and
aggregates information over time and it solves problems. You’ve
heard the expression just give me the cold hard facts. That is what
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	15
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 18 --

is meant by cold cognition, the labor intensive process of combing
through facts and building a decision matrix.
You can trigger a hot cognition instantly. You can trigger a
wanting even though you’re delivering the price. Cold cognition,
analysis can take hours or days and most presentations,
especially the point where they deliver price, are set up to take
the audience down the path of cold cognition to try to justify the
price with facts and information.
So look, let’s summarize it like this. It’s really basic. You can
achieve a hot cognition around price and delivery terms in your
audience by stacking four frames quickly one after the other. You
can help your audience or your buyer in his mind achieve wanting,
even through the price and the commercial terms section of your
pitch or your closing you can still have that feeling of wanting if
you stack those frames and create that hot cognition.
Now that we’ve talked quite a bit about why hot cognition is
important and how in theory to stack four frames to get that hot
cognition, let’s talk more specifically about how to do it in your
business. So I think there’s five steps. First of all, write your Time
Frame, your Intrigue Frame, your Prize Frame and your Moral
Authority Frame. Write each of those on an index card and make
sure to note on each one which of the commercial terms you’re
going to use in that frame. So, for example, for me in the Time
Frame I used that to deliver, clearly the order date, that’s an easy
one. The Time Frame you will use to deliver the final order date or
if you’re closing a deal, so the deal has to be closed by June 30th,
October 15th, whatever it is. So that will be your Time Frame.
Your Moral Authority Frame, only you will know what that is in
your business. Usually I will use the Moral Authority Frame to tie it
all together. The Intrigue Frame can be very good for delivering the
price. The price is $1,000, the price is $100,000, the price is $5
million. What’s very interesting about this is XYZ. Use the Intrigue
Frame to deliver price. It’s very useful. The Prize Frame can be
used to deliver lots of commercial terms such as the minimum
order volume, I only work with people who order 100,000 crates
of ball bearing or more because I’ve been doing this for ten years
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	16
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 18 --

and I work with big customers. OK? Easy. Prize Frame around
order volume. So Time Frame, Moral Authority Frame, Intrigue
Frame, Prize Frame. Those will be the four that you stack. Just
write them on an index card.
Step two will be practice. You’ve got to flip through those cards
and deliver pretty smoothly because you’re delivering a lot of
information and commercial terms all at once. So make sure you
can go through that whole four-frame stack smoothly in a way
that creates wanting. So it’s practice, going through it, having the
cards, going through it and practice and making sure you’re
smooth.
Now step three is going to surprise you because it’s so easy. After
you’ve delivered the four-frame stack, it’s time to do the
following; withdraw. Get out of there. You have done everything
you can do and you need to start moving backwards. I think I’ve
said it three or four times already in just this module. People
chase that which moves away from them. We want that which we
cannot have and we only value those things that we work for.
You’ve done as much as you can possibly do. Do not lead in and
be needy. Start withdrawing.
I’ll give you in the next step a way to do that, but in step three
you’re going to start withdrawing, literally start moving
backwards. Step four is to leave the room. Now bring the buy with
you or bring the audience with you but go somewhere else. That’s
called a location shift. It is now time and there’s many, many
reasons beyond the scope of what we can get into here. It is now
time to do a location shift. Withdraw and then leave the room and
then take them with you. If you can get them to tour the factory
or go see the sunshine outside or go to the break room or go get a
cup of coffee or go out to lunch or whatever it is, withdraw. You
can keep them with you and you can continue talking about the
deal, but outside of that pitching environment. I cannot
emphasize this enough. Create a location shift. Withdraw and
then the final step is to leave.
Now step five in this process is to remember that you still have to
nail the hook point so you’re going to move smoothly into the
O: Offer the Prize
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	17
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 18 --

next module and that’s what we’ll be doing next. So go through
the workbook, obviously, on Offer the Prize and these steps will be
laid out. Get your index cards going with your Time Frame, your
Moral Authority Frame, your Intrigue Frame and your Prize Frame.
Get good at delivering that smoothly. When you’re done,
remember to withdraw and then make a location shift. When you
accomplished that, you’ve done it as perfectly as you can.
O: Offer the Prize

-- 18 of 18 --
